  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}

Riemann surfaces

A Riemann surface is a one-complex-dimensional manifold — equivalently, a real surface with complex-analytic transition maps. The subject is where complex analysis, algebraic geometry, and low-dimensional topology become the same story told in three dialects.

blocks.html:
1. What is a Riemann surface?

A Riemann surface $X$ is a connected Hausdorff space equipped with an atlas of charts $\varphi_\alpha\colon U_\alpha\to V_\alpha\subset\mathbb{C}$ whose transition maps $\varphi_\beta\circ\varphi_\alpha^{-1}$ are biholomorphic wherever defined. Since a biholomorphism of open sets in $\mathbb{C}\cong\mathbb{R}^2$ is in particular a smooth orientation-preserving diffeomorphism, every Riemann surface is canonically an oriented real $2$-manifold.

Two perspectives sit on top of this definition and will not go away:

  • Analytic. A Riemann surface is exactly the natural domain for multi-valued holomorphic functions: the locus where $\sqrt{z}$, $\log z$, or $\wp$-inverses become single-valued and well-behaved.
  • Algebraic. A compact Riemann surface is the same thing as a smooth projective algebraic curve over $\mathbb{C}$. The functor from one to the other is an equivalence of categories; in either direction, meromorphic functions correspond to rational functions.

The miracle that makes the subject work is that on a compact Riemann surface, the complex-analytic structure rigidifies the topology: there are only countably many Riemann-surface structures on a surface of genus $g\ge 2$ (Teichmüller's theorem gives the correct count — a $(6g-6)$-real-dimensional moduli space), but the genus itself is a pure topological invariant, computed by $\chi = 2 - 2g$.

The right mental image of a Riemann surface is a donut (or pretzel, or sphere) that knows what it means for a function on it to be holomorphic. Almost every deep theorem in the subject is about how this local notion of holomorphy pins down global data: cohomology, degrees of line bundles, dimensions of spaces of meromorphic functions.

Why the term "complex curve"?

Complex dimension $1$ is real dimension $2$, so when algebraic geometers call it a curve they mean the complex-analytic dimension. A genus-$2$ surface — a two-holed donut to a topologist — is a "curve" to an algebraic geometer. Live with the terminology; it rewards you.

The basic invariants and where they live

For a compact Riemann surface $X$ of genus $g$ the fundamental invariants we will compute or cite below are:

invariantvaluecharacter
genus $g$$g \ge 0$topological (and hence conformal)
Euler characteristic $\chi$$2-2g$topological
$\dim H^0(X,\Omega^1)$$g$complex-analytic (Hodge)
$\deg K_X$$2g-2$algebro-geometric (canonical divisor)
$\dim \mathcal{M}_g$ (for $g\ge 2$)$3g-3$ (complex)moduli-theoretic

The pattern you should notice: every single complex-analytic number is a function of the topological invariant $g$ alone. Everything below is machinery to make that statement precise, and to turn it into effective computations.

See also
  • Holomorphic functions
  • Smooth manifolds and atlases
Used in
Elliptic curves · Complex uniformization and CM
Riemann surfaces · Branched covers and maps
Riemann surfaces · Genus as the universal invariant

2. The simplest examples: sphere and torus

The Riemann sphere $\mathbb{CP}^1$

Glue two copies of $\mathbb{C}$ along $\mathbb{C}^\times$ by the transition $w = 1/z$. The result is topologically $S^2$ and is called the Riemann sphere; with coordinates it is the complex projective line $\mathbb{CP}^1$. The point added to $\mathbb{C}$ at infinity is genuinely a point of $X$ once you do this — in the $w$-chart it is just $w = 0$.
Stereographic chart · sphere vs plane drag the point in the plane

The two charts cover $\mathbb{CP}^1$, their intersection is $\mathbb{C}^\times$ in either picture, and the transition $w = 1/z$ is visibly biholomorphic there. Stereographic projection from the north pole gives the map $z \leftrightarrow $ point of the sphere concretely; as $|z|\to\infty$ the corresponding point on the sphere approaches the north pole, which is the point $w = 0$ in the other chart.

(function(){
  const svg = document.getElementById('svg-sphere');
  const out = document.getElementById('out-sphere');
  if(!svg || !out) return;

  // plane on left, sphere on right
  const planeCX = 170, planeCY = 150, planeR = 110;
  const sphX = 540, sphY = 150, sphR = 100;

  // plane frame
  const planeBox = SVG('rect',{x:planeCX-planeR-10,y:planeCY-planeR-10,width:2*planeR+20,height:2*planeR+20,
    fill:'#0b0f16',stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1,rx:6});
  svg.appendChild(planeBox);
  // axes
  svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:planeCX-planeR,y1:planeCY,x2:planeCX+planeR,y2:planeCY,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
  svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:planeCX,y1:planeCY-planeR,x2:planeCX,y2:planeCY+planeR,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
  // unit circle on plane
  svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:planeCX,cy:planeCY,r:planeR*0.35,
    fill:'none',stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-dasharray':'3 3'}));
  // labels
  const planeTitle = SVG('text',{x:planeCX,y:planeCY-planeR-20,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,fill:'var(--mute)'});
  planeTitle.textContent = 'z-chart: C'; svg.appendChild(planeTitle);
  const planeAx = SVG('text',{x:planeCX+planeR+6,y:planeCY+4,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'});
  planeAx.textContent = 'Re'; svg.appendChild(planeAx);
  const planeAy = SVG('text',{x:planeCX+4,y:planeCY-planeR-2,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'});
  planeAy.textContent = 'Im'; svg.appendChild(planeAy);

  // sphere
  svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:sphX,cy:sphY,r:sphR,fill:'#101722',stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1.2}));
  // equator
  svg.appendChild(SVG('ellipse',{cx:sphX,cy:sphY,rx:sphR,ry:sphR*0.3,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-dasharray':'3 3'}));
  // meridian
  svg.appendChild(SVG('ellipse',{cx:sphX,cy:sphY,rx:sphR*0.3,ry:sphR,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-dasharray':'3 3'}));
  // north/south poles
  svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:sphX,cy:sphY-sphR,r:3,fill:'var(--pink)'}));
  svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:sphX,cy:sphY+sphR,r:3,fill:'var(--cyan)'}));
  const nLbl = SVG('text',{x:sphX+8,y:sphY-sphR+2,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--pink)'});
  nLbl.textContent = 'N = ∞'; svg.appendChild(nLbl);
  const sLbl = SVG('text',{x:sphX+8,y:sphY+sphR+2,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--cyan)'});
  sLbl.textContent = 'S = 0'; svg.appendChild(sLbl);
  const sTitle = SVG('text',{x:sphX,y:sphY-sphR-20,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,fill:'var(--mute)'});
  sTitle.textContent = 'CP¹ = S²'; svg.appendChild(sTitle);

  // draggable point on plane
  const pt = SVG('circle',{cx:planeCX+30,cy:planeCY-20,r:6,fill:'var(--yellow)',stroke:'#111','stroke-width':1.5,style:'cursor:grab'});
  svg.appendChild(pt);
  // corresponding point on sphere
  const ptS = SVG('circle',{r:6,fill:'var(--yellow)',stroke:'#111','stroke-width':1.5});
  svg.appendChild(ptS);

  function spherePos(px, py){
    // complex z = (px - cx)/planeR * scale,  use scale = 1 so unit disk is 35% of radius? We'll use a different mapping:
    // let normalized u = (px-planeCX)/(planeR*0.35), same v. Then (u,v)=z. Stereographic from north:
    //   X = 2u/(1+u²+v²), Y = 2v/(1+u²+v²), Z = (u²+v²-1)/(1+u²+v²).
    const u = (px - planeCX)/(planeR*0.35);
    const v = -(py - planeCY)/(planeR*0.35); // flip y for math convention
    const d = 1 + u*u + v*v;
    const X = 2*u/d, Y = 2*v/d, Z = (u*u + v*v - 1)/d;
    // project sphere to screen: (X,Y,Z) with Z up, Y into screen (isometric-ish).
    // screen x = sphX + sphR * X;   screen y = sphY - sphR*Z + sphR*0.25*Y (slight tilt)
    const sx = sphX + sphR * X * 1.0;
    const sy = sphY - sphR * Z + sphR*0.18 * Y;
    return {sx, sy, u, v};
  }

  function update(){
    const px = +pt.getAttribute('cx'), py = +pt.getAttribute('cy');
    const {sx,sy,u,v} = spherePos(px,py);
    ptS.setAttribute('cx', sx); ptS.setAttribute('cy', sy);
    const mod = Math.hypot(u,v);
    const zStr = `${u.toFixed(3)} ${v>=0?'+':'−'} ${Math.abs(v).toFixed(3)} i`;
    const wMod = mod > 1e-9 ? (1/mod).toFixed(3) : '∞';
    out.textContent =
      `z = ${zStr}     |z| = ${mod.toFixed(3)}\n` +
      `w = 1/z        |w| = ${wMod}\n` +
      (mod > 8 ? 'near north pole: best to describe in w-chart' :
       mod < 0.12 ? 'near south pole: z-chart is best here' :
       'both charts are fine in this region');
  }

  // dragging
  let dragging = false;
  pt.addEventListener('mousedown', e => { dragging = true; e.preventDefault(); });
  svg.addEventListener('mousemove', e => {
    if(!dragging) return;
    const r = svg.getBoundingClientRect();
    // map to svg coords (viewBox 760x300)
    const sx = (e.clientX - r.left) * (760/r.width);
    const sy = (e.clientY - r.top) * (300/r.height);
    // clamp into plane box
    const minX = planeCX - planeR, maxX = planeCX + planeR;
    const minY = planeCY - planeR, maxY = planeCY + planeR;
    pt.setAttribute('cx', Math.max(minX, Math.min(maxX, sx)));
    pt.setAttribute('cy', Math.max(minY, Math.min(maxY, sy)));
    update();
  });
  window.addEventListener('mouseup', () => { dragging = false; });
  pt.addEventListener('touchstart', e => { dragging = true; });
  svg.addEventListener('touchmove', e => {
    if(!dragging || !e.touches[0]) return;
    const r = svg.getBoundingClientRect();
    const t = e.touches[0];
    const sx = (t.clientX - r.left) * (760/r.width);
    const sy = (t.clientY - r.top) * (300/r.height);
    const minX = planeCX - planeR, maxX = planeCX + planeR;
    const minY = planeCY - planeR, maxY = planeCY + planeR;
    pt.setAttribute('cx', Math.max(minX, Math.min(maxX, sx)));
    pt.setAttribute('cy', Math.max(minY, Math.min(maxY, sy)));
    update();
    e.preventDefault();
  }, {passive:false});
  window.addEventListener('touchend', () => { dragging = false; });
  update();
})();
</script>

The torus $\mathbb{C}/\Lambda$

Pick a lattice $\Lambda = \mathbb{Z}\omega_1 \oplus \mathbb{Z}\omega_2$ with $\omega_1,\omega_2\in\mathbb{C}$ linearly independent over $\mathbb{R}$. The quotient $\mathbb{C}/\Lambda$ inherits charts from $\mathbb{C}$ because translations are biholomorphic; topologically it is a torus. The conformal class of this torus depends only on the modular parameter $\tau = \omega_2/\omega_1$, and we may always arrange $\mathrm{Im}\,\tau > 0$ by swapping $\omega_1\leftrightarrow \omega_2$ if necessary. Two lattices give biholomorphic tori iff their $\tau$'s are related by the $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ action $\tau\mapsto \frac{a\tau+b}{c\tau+d}$, so the moduli space of complex tori is $\mathbb{H}/\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$.
Lattice, parallelogram, and τ drag the tips of ω₁ (yellow) and ω₂ (blue)

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = document.getElementById('svg-torus');
  const out = document.getElementById('out-torus');
  if(!svg || !out) return;

  // left: C with lattice & parallelogram; right: H with τ.
  const cCX = 190, cCY = 180, cR = 120;
  const hCX = 540, hCY = 210, hW = 180;
  const hBaseY = hCY + 80; // real axis
  // frames
  svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:cCX-cR-10,y:cCY-cR-10,width:2*cR+20,height:2*cR+20,
    fill:'#0b0f16',stroke:'var(--line)',rx:6}));
  svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:hCX-hW,y:hCY-120,width:2*hW,height:200,
    fill:'#0b0f16',stroke:'var(--line)',rx:6}));
  // axes on left
  svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:cCX-cR,y1:cCY,x2:cCX+cR,y2:cCY,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
  svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:cCX,y1:cCY-cR,x2:cCX,y2:cCY+cR,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
  const t1 = SVG('text',{x:cCX,y:cCY-cR-22,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,fill:'var(--mute)'});
  t1.textContent = 'C — lattice basis'; svg.appendChild(t1);

  // upper half-plane right
  svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:hCX-hW,y1:hBaseY,x2:hCX+hW,y2:hBaseY,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1.2}));
  const t2 = SVG('text',{x:hCX,y:hCY-120-8,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,fill:'var(--mute)'});
  t2.textContent = 'upper half-plane H — τ = ω₂/ω₁'; svg.appendChild(t2);
  // fundamental domain boundary
  // |τ|=1 arc (from (-1/2, √3/2) to (1/2, √3/2))
  // in screen coords: τ_screen_x = hCX + scale*Re(τ), y = hBaseY - scale*Im(τ)
  const sc = 80;
  const domainPath = SVG('path',{
    d:`M${hCX-sc*0.5} ${hBaseY-sc*Math.sqrt(3)/2}
       A${sc} ${sc} 0 0 0 ${hCX+sc*0.5} ${hBaseY-sc*Math.sqrt(3)/2}
       L${hCX+sc*0.5} ${hBaseY-180}
       L${hCX-sc*0.5} ${hBaseY-180} Z`,
    fill:'rgba(131,193,103,0.08)',stroke:'var(--green)','stroke-width':1,'stroke-dasharray':'4 3'});
  svg.appendChild(domainPath);

  // draggable tips for ω₁ (yellow) and ω₂ (blue)
  let w1 = {x: 70, y: 0};    // complex units scale
  let w2 = {x: 25, y: 60};
  const w1dot = SVG('circle',{r:6,fill:'var(--yellow)',stroke:'#111','stroke-width':1.5,style:'cursor:grab'});
  const w2dot = SVG('circle',{r:6,fill:'var(--blue)',stroke:'#111','stroke-width':1.5,style:'cursor:grab'});
  // parallelogram group
  const poly = SVG('path',{fill:'rgba(255,216,102,0.08)',stroke:'var(--ink)','stroke-width':1.2});
  const w1line = SVG('line',{stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-width':2});
  const w2line = SVG('line',{stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':2});
  svg.appendChild(poly); svg.appendChild(w1line); svg.appendChild(w2line);
  svg.appendChild(w1dot); svg.appendChild(w2dot);

  // edge-identification arrows (draw small arrowheads along midpoints)
  const idEdgeA1 = SVG('path',{fill:'none',stroke:'var(--green)','stroke-width':1.5,'marker-end':'url(#tarra)'});
  const idEdgeA2 = SVG('path',{fill:'none',stroke:'var(--green)','stroke-width':1.5,'marker-end':'url(#tarra)'});
  const idEdgeB1 = SVG('path',{fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':1.5,'marker-end':'url(#tarrb)'});
  const idEdgeB2 = SVG('path',{fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':1.5,'marker-end':'url(#tarrb)'});
  svg.appendChild(idEdgeA1); svg.appendChild(idEdgeA2);
  svg.appendChild(idEdgeB1); svg.appendChild(idEdgeB2);
  ensureArrow(svg,'tarra','var(--green)');
  ensureArrow(svg,'tarrb','var(--pink)');

  // τ dot in H
  const tauDot = SVG('circle',{r:6,fill:'var(--violet)',stroke:'#111','stroke-width':1.5});
  svg.appendChild(tauDot);

  function update(){
    // scale factor: 1 complex unit = 1 screen unit already; keep it small
    const scl = 1.0;
    const p0 = {x: cCX, y: cCY};
    const p1 = {x: cCX + w1.x*scl, y: cCY - w1.y*scl};
    const p2 = {x: cCX + w2.x*scl, y: cCY - w2.y*scl};
    const p12 = {x: p1.x + (p2.x - p0.x), y: p1.y + (p2.y - p0.y)};
    poly.setAttribute('d',`M${p0.x} ${p0.y} L${p1.x} ${p1.y} L${p12.x} ${p12.y} L${p2.x} ${p2.y} Z`);
    w1line.setAttribute('x1',p0.x); w1line.setAttribute('y1',p0.y);
    w1line.setAttribute('x2',p1.x); w1line.setAttribute('y2',p1.y);
    w2line.setAttribute('x1',p0.x); w2line.setAttribute('y1',p0.y);
    w2line.setAttribute('x2',p2.x); w2line.setAttribute('y2',p2.y);
    w1dot.setAttribute('cx',p1.x); w1dot.setAttribute('cy',p1.y);
    w2dot.setAttribute('cx',p2.x); w2dot.setAttribute('cy',p2.y);

    // identification arrows on opposite edges (green = ω₁ edges, pink = ω₂ edges)
    const midA1 = {x:(p0.x+p1.x)/2, y:(p0.y+p1.y)/2};
    const midA2 = {x:(p2.x+p12.x)/2, y:(p2.y+p12.y)/2};
    const offA = {x:(p1.x-p0.x)*0.15, y:(p1.y-p0.y)*0.15};
    idEdgeA1.setAttribute('d',`M${midA1.x-offA.x} ${midA1.y-offA.y} L${midA1.x+offA.x} ${midA1.y+offA.y}`);
    idEdgeA2.setAttribute('d',`M${midA2.x-offA.x} ${midA2.y-offA.y} L${midA2.x+offA.x} ${midA2.y+offA.y}`);
    const midB1 = {x:(p0.x+p2.x)/2, y:(p0.y+p2.y)/2};
    const midB2 = {x:(p1.x+p12.x)/2, y:(p1.y+p12.y)/2};
    const offB = {x:(p2.x-p0.x)*0.15, y:(p2.y-p0.y)*0.15};
    idEdgeB1.setAttribute('d',`M${midB1.x-offB.x} ${midB1.y-offB.y} L${midB1.x+offB.x} ${midB1.y+offB.y}`);
    idEdgeB2.setAttribute('d',`M${midB2.x-offB.x} ${midB2.y-offB.y} L${midB2.x+offB.x} ${midB2.y+offB.y}`);

    // τ = ω₂ / ω₁ ; here we treat w.x+iw.y as a complex number
    const a = w1.x, b = w1.y, c = w2.x, d = w2.y;
    const den = a*a + b*b;
    let tRe = (a*c + b*d)/den;
    let tIm = (a*d - b*c)/den;
    let swapped = false;
    if(tIm < 0){ tRe = -tRe; tIm = -tIm; swapped = true; } // τ -> -τ means swap basis
    // plot τ in H
    const scl2 = 80;
    const txS = hCX + scl2*tRe;
    const tyS = hBaseY - scl2*tIm;
    tauDot.setAttribute('cx',txS); tauDot.setAttribute('cy',tyS);
    // clamp if off box
    const inBoxX = txS >= hCX-hW+4 && txS <= hCX+hW-4;
    const inBoxY = tyS >= hCY-120+4 && tyS <= hBaseY-4;
    tauDot.setAttribute('opacity', inBoxX && inBoxY ? 1 : 0.25);

    // Check fundamental domain: |τ|≥1, |Re τ|≤1/2, Im τ>0
    const inFD = (tRe*tRe + tIm*tIm >= 1 - 1e-6) && (Math.abs(tRe) <= 0.5 + 1e-6);
    const statusColor = inFD ? 'var(--green)' : 'var(--yellow)';
    out.style.color = statusColor;
    const tauStr = `${tRe.toFixed(3)} ${tIm>=0?'+':'−'} ${Math.abs(tIm).toFixed(3)} i`;
    out.textContent =
      `ω₁ = ${a.toFixed(2)} + ${b.toFixed(2)} i    ω₂ = ${c.toFixed(2)} + ${d.toFixed(2)} i\n` +
      (swapped ? '(basis swapped so Im τ > 0)\n' : '') +
      `τ  = ω₂ / ω₁ = ${tauStr}\n` +
      (inFD
        ? 'τ lies in standard fundamental domain |τ|≥1, |Re τ|≤½'
        : 'τ outside standard fundamental domain — apply an SL₂(Z) move');
  }

  function bindDrag(dot, which){
    let dragging=false;
    dot.addEventListener('mousedown', e => { dragging=true; e.preventDefault(); });
    svg.addEventListener('mousemove', e => {
      if(!dragging) return;
      const r = svg.getBoundingClientRect();
      const sx = (e.clientX-r.left)*(760/r.width);
      const sy = (e.clientY-r.top)*(320/r.height);
      const dx = sx - cCX, dy = -(sy - cCY);
      // clamp into circle-ish area
      const mag = Math.hypot(dx,dy);
      const cap = cR - 6;
      let nx = dx, ny = dy;
      if(mag > cap){ nx = dx/mag*cap; ny = dy/mag*cap; }
      if(which===1){ w1 = {x:nx, y:ny}; }
      else         { w2 = {x:nx, y:ny}; }
      update();
    });
    window.addEventListener('mouseup', () => { dragging=false; });
    dot.addEventListener('touchstart', e => { dragging=true; });
    svg.addEventListener('touchmove', e => {
      if(!dragging || !e.touches[0]) return;
      const r = svg.getBoundingClientRect();
      const t = e.touches[0];
      const sx = (t.clientX-r.left)*(760/r.width);
      const sy = (t.clientY-r.top)*(320/r.height);
      const dx = sx - cCX, dy = -(sy - cCY);
      const mag = Math.hypot(dx,dy);
      const cap = cR - 6;
      let nx = dx, ny = dy;
      if(mag > cap){ nx = dx/mag*cap; ny = dy/mag*cap; }
      if(which===1){ w1 = {x:nx, y:ny}; }
      else         { w2 = {x:nx, y:ny}; }
      update();
      e.preventDefault();
    }, {passive:false});
    window.addEventListener('touchend', () => { dragging=false; });
  }
  bindDrag(w1dot, 1);
  bindDrag(w2dot, 2);
  update();
})();
</script>

The Weierstrass $\wp$-function supplies an explicit biholomorphism $\mathbb{C}/\Lambda \xrightarrow{\sim} E_\Lambda\subset\mathbb{CP}^2$ onto the elliptic curve $y^2 = 4x^3 - g_2 x - g_3$, matching the complex-analytic torus to a smooth plane cubic. This is the first and cleanest example of "Riemann surfaces = algebraic curves."

Why only two charts suffice for the sphere

In general an atlas on a Riemann surface can have many charts, but $\mathbb{CP}^1$ admits one with just two. That is because $\mathbb{CP}^1\smallsetminus\{\infty\}$ is biholomorphic to $\mathbb{C}$, which is itself a chart — the obstruction to having a single global chart is precisely the topology (a sphere is not contractible). The general statement is that $X$ is biholomorphic to an open subset of $\mathbb{C}$ iff $X$ is simply connected and non-compact — part of the conclusion of uniformization, §5.

3. Branched covers from $w^2 = P(z)$

Let $P(z)\in\mathbb{C}[z]$ be a squarefree polynomial of degree $d$. The equation $w^2 = P(z)$ defines a curve whose smooth model is a compact Riemann surface $X$ admitting a map $\pi\colon X\to \mathbb{CP}^1$, $(z,w)\mapsto z$. Away from the roots of $P$ (and, if $d$ is odd, also $\infty$) the map $\pi$ is a $2$-to-$1$ local biholomorphism. Over a root $z_0$ of $P$ we have $w=0$ and the two sheets come together: $z_0$ is a branch point.

The standard picture is a two-sheeted cover of $\mathbb{CP}^1$ glued along branch cuts joining pairs of branch points. A small loop around an odd number of branch points swaps sheets; a loop around an even number returns to the same sheet. This is the monodromy of the cover.

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = document.getElementById('svg-branch');
  const out = document.getElementById('out-branch');
  const pick = document.getElementById('branch-pick');
  const lamWrap = document.getElementById('branch-lam-wrap');
  const lamSl = document.getElementById('branch-lam');
  const lamVal = document.getElementById('branch-lam-val');
  if(!svg || !out) return;

  const cx = 380, cy = 150;
  const scale = 60;

  function branches(){
    const v = pick.value;
    if(v==='sqrt')     return [{re:0,im:0,label:'0'}, {re:null,im:null,label:'∞'}];
    if(v==='z2m1')     return [{re:-1,im:0,label:'−1'}, {re:1,im:0,label:'1'}];
    const lam = parseFloat(lamSl.value);
    return [{re:0,im:0,label:'0'}, {re:1,im:0,label:'1'}, {re:lam,im:0,label:'λ'}, {re:null,im:null,label:'∞'}];
  }

  // loop is a circle in z-plane the user can drag
  let loopC = {x: 0.3, y: 0.3, r: 0.8};

  function render(){
    svg.innerHTML = '';
    // frame
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:cx-280,y:cy-120,width:560,height:260,
      fill:'#0b0f16',stroke:'var(--line)',rx:6}));
    // axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:cx-260,y1:cy,x2:cx+260,y2:cy,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:cx,y1:cy-110,x2:cx,y2:cy+110,stroke:'var(--line)'}));

    const bs = branches();
    const finites = bs.filter(b=>b.re!==null);
    // draw branch points
    for(const b of bs){
      if(b.re===null){
        // draw ∞ marker at top corner
        svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:cx+240,cy:cy-95,r:5,fill:'var(--pink)'}));
        const lb = SVG('text',{x:cx+230,y:cy-100,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':11,fill:'var(--pink)'});
        lb.textContent = '∞ (branch)'; svg.appendChild(lb);
      } else {
        const px = cx + b.re*scale, py = cy - b.im*scale;
        svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:px,cy:py,r:6,fill:'var(--pink)',stroke:'#111','stroke-width':1.5}));
        const lb = SVG('text',{x:px+8,y:py-8,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--pink)'});
        lb.textContent = b.label; svg.appendChild(lb);
      }
    }
    // draw branch cuts (pair them up greedily)
    // finite pairs; if an odd number of finites, pair last with ∞ off to the right
    const pts = finites.map(b=>({x:cx+b.re*scale,y:cy-b.im*scale}));
    const pairs = [];
    for(let i=0;i+1<pts.length;i+=2) pairs.push([pts[i],pts[i+1]]);
    if(pts.length%2===1){ pairs.push([pts[pts.length-1], {x:cx+240,y:cy-95}]); }
    if(finites.length===0 && bs.length===2){
      // w² = z case: cut from 0 to ∞
      pairs.push([{x:cx,y:cy},{x:cx+240,y:cy-95}]);
    }
    for(const [a,b] of pairs){
      svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{
        d:`M${a.x} ${a.y} L${b.x} ${b.y}`,
        stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-width':3,'stroke-linecap':'round',opacity:0.7}));
    }

    // draw the draggable loop
    const lx = cx + loopC.x*scale;
    const ly = cy - loopC.y*scale;
    const lr = Math.abs(loopC.r)*scale;
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:lx,cy:ly,r:lr,
      fill:'rgba(88,196,221,0.08)',stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':2,'stroke-dasharray':'4 3'}));
    // center handle
    const h = SVG('circle',{cx:lx,cy:ly,r:5,fill:'var(--blue)',stroke:'#111','stroke-width':1.5,style:'cursor:grab',id:'loop-h'});
    svg.appendChild(h);
    // radius handle
    const rh = SVG('circle',{cx:lx+lr,cy:ly,r:5,fill:'var(--cyan)',stroke:'#111','stroke-width':1.5,style:'cursor:ew-resize',id:'loop-rh'});
    svg.appendChild(rh);

    // count branch points inside loop
    let inside = 0;
    for(const b of finites){
      const dx = b.re - loopC.x, dy = b.im - loopC.y;
      if(dx*dx+dy*dy < loopC.r*loopC.r) inside++;
    }
    // include ∞: if sum of all-finite-branches is odd total (odd # of total branch points odd → ∞ is a branch),
    // then "outside loop enclosing ∞" contributes. Simpler rule: ∞ is in the complement of any bounded loop; it's "outside".
    const totalBr = bs.length;
    const infIsBranch = bs.some(b=>b.re===null);
    // Monodromy: sheet swap iff odd # of enclosed branch points (counting those inside the loop).
    const swap = (inside % 2) === 1;

    // genus via Riemann–Hurwitz: g = (total_branch_points / 2) − 1 for a double cover of P¹
    const g = totalBr/2 - 1;

    // title labels
    const t = SVG('text',{x:cx,y:cy-120-6,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    t.textContent = 'z-plane — two sheets glued along yellow cuts'; svg.appendChild(t);

    // sheet indicator boxes
    const aStr = SVG('text',{x:cx-250,y:cy+125,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    aStr.textContent = `branch points: ${totalBr}   inside loop: ${inside}`; svg.appendChild(aStr);
    const bStr = SVG('text',{x:cx+250,y:cy+125,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':12,
      fill: swap ? 'var(--pink)' : 'var(--green)'});
    bStr.textContent = swap ? 'loop swaps sheets (σ)' : 'loop returns to same sheet (id)'; svg.appendChild(bStr);

    out.textContent =
      `total branch points (incl. ∞ if needed): ${totalBr}\n` +
      `inside loop: ${inside}   →   monodromy: ${swap ? 'sheet-swap' : 'identity'}\n` +
      `Riemann–Hurwitz gives g(X) = ${totalBr/2} − 1 = ${g}`;
    out.style.color = 'var(--ink)';

    // hook up drags
    let dc=false, dr=false;
    h.addEventListener('mousedown', e => { dc=true; e.preventDefault(); });
    rh.addEventListener('mousedown', e => { dr=true; e.preventDefault(); });
    svg.addEventListener('mousemove', e => {
      if(!dc && !dr) return;
      const R = svg.getBoundingClientRect();
      const sx = (e.clientX-R.left)*(760/R.width);
      const sy = (e.clientY-R.top)*(300/R.height);
      if(dc){
        loopC.x = (sx - cx)/scale;
        loopC.y = -(sy - cy)/scale;
      } else if(dr){
        const dx = (sx - cx)/scale - loopC.x;
        const dy = -(sy - cy)/scale - loopC.y;
        loopC.r = Math.max(0.1, Math.hypot(dx,dy));
      }
      render();
    });
    window.addEventListener('mouseup', () => { dc=false; dr=false; });
  }

  pick.addEventListener('change', () => {
    lamWrap.style.display = pick.value==='legendre' ? '' : 'none';
    render();
  });
  lamSl.addEventListener('input', () => {
    lamVal.textContent = parseFloat(lamSl.value).toFixed(2);
    render();
  });
  render();
})();
</script>

For the hyperelliptic family $w^2 = P(z)$ with $\deg P = d$, the number of branch points is $d$ (plus $\infty$ if $d$ is odd). Riemann–Hurwitz will give genus $\lceil (d-1)/2\rceil$, so: $d=2$ gives a sphere, $d=3,4$ give a torus, $d=5,6$ give genus $2$, and so on. The Legendre form $w^2 = z(z-1)(z-\lambda)$ is an elliptic curve for every $\lambda\in\mathbb{C}\smallsetminus\{0,1\}$; different $\lambda$ give different complex structures, related by the $\Gamma(2)$ action.

Monodromy, more seriously

The complement $\mathbb{CP}^1\smallsetminus B$ of the branch locus $B = \{b_1,\ldots,b_r\}$ has fundamental group freely generated by small loops $\gamma_1,\ldots,\gamma_r$ around the $b_i$, modulo the single relation $\gamma_1\cdots\gamma_r = 1$. The unramified cover $X\smallsetminus \pi^{-1}(B)\to \mathbb{CP}^1\smallsetminus B$ is classified by the monodromy representation $\rho\colon \pi_1(\mathbb{CP}^1\smallsetminus B, *) \to S_n$, where $n$ is the degree of the cover. For double covers $n=2$, so $\rho$ lands in $S_2 = \{1,\sigma\}$, and $\rho(\gamma_i) = \sigma$ at each branch point. The relation $\prod\gamma_i = 1$ becomes $\sigma^r = 1$, which forces $r$ to be even.

For higher-degree covers the local contribution is the cycle structure of $\rho(\gamma_i)$. This is the same data as specifying the partition of $n$ given by the ramification indices over $b_i$ — exactly the numbers that feed into the Riemann–Hurwitz calculator in §4.

Used in
Riemann surfaces · Genus as the universal invariant
Riemann surfaces · Riemann–Hurwitz and uniformization

4. Riemann–Hurwitz and genus

Let $f\colon X\to Y$ be a non-constant holomorphic map of compact Riemann surfaces, of degree $n$. At each point $p\in X$ there is a local form $z \mapsto z^{e_p}$ in suitable coordinates; the integer $e_p\ge 1$ is the ramification index at $p$. The set of $p$ with $e_p > 1$ is finite.

The Riemann–Hurwitz formula: $$ 2g_X - 2 \;=\; n\,(2g_Y - 2) \;+\; \sum_{p\in X}(e_p - 1). $$ Equivalently, $\chi(X) = n\,\chi(Y) - \sum(e_p - 1)$: each ramification eats one of the $n$ Euler-characteristic copies.

Two sanity checks:

  • Unramified cover. If $e_p \equiv 1$, the ramification sum is $0$ and $\chi(X) = n\chi(Y)$. This is just the covering-space relation: an $n$-fold cover multiplies Euler characteristic by $n$.
  • Double cover of $\mathbb{CP}^1$. $n=2$, $g_Y=0$, each ramified point has $e_p=2$ contributing $1$. With $b$ branch points we get $2g_X - 2 = 2(-2) + b$, i.e. $g_X = b/2 - 1$. So $b$ must be even (and it is, by the topology of the cover).

Riemann–Hurwitz calculator enter degree, base genus, ramification indices

<script>
(function(){
  const nEl = document.getElementById('rh-n');
  const gyEl = document.getElementById('rh-gy');
  const epsEl = document.getElementById('rh-eps');
  const go = document.getElementById('rh-go');
  const out = document.getElementById('out-rh');
  const exBtns = document.querySelectorAll('#w-rh [data-ex]');
  if(!out) return;

  const examples = {
    hyper6: {n:2, gy:0, eps:'2,2,2,2,2,2',
      note:'six simple branch points → genus 2 hyperelliptic curve'},
    cubic:  {n:3, gy:0, eps:'2,2,2,2,2,2',
      note:'a degree-3 cover of P¹ with 6 simple (e=2) ramification points → genus 2 trigonal curve'},
    ellcover:{n:2, gy:1, eps:'',
      note:'unramified double cover of a torus → genus 2 surface (this is how bielliptic curves arise)'},
    exotic: {n:4, gy:1, eps:'3,3,2',
      note:'exotic mixed ramification on a torus base'}
  };

  function compute(){
    const n = parseInt(nEl.value,10);
    const gy = parseInt(gyEl.value,10);
    const raw = (epsEl.value||'').split(',').map(s=>s.trim()).filter(Boolean);
    const eps = raw.map(s=>parseInt(s,10)).filter(x=>x>=1 && Number.isFinite(x));
    const sum = eps.reduce((a,x)=>a+(x-1),0);
    const chiY = 2 - 2*gy;
    const chiX = n*chiY - sum;
    const halfRam = sum/2;
    const gx = 1 + n*(gy - 1) + halfRam;
    let lines = [];
    lines.push(`n = ${n}   g(Y) = ${gy}   ramification indices = [${eps.join(', ')||'—'}]`);
    lines.push(`Σ (e_p − 1) = ${sum}`);
    lines.push(`χ(X) = n·χ(Y) − Σ (e_p−1) = ${n}·${chiY} − ${sum} = ${chiX}`);
    lines.push(`g(X) = 1 + n(g(Y)−1) + ½ Σ (e_p−1) = 1 + ${n}·${gy-1} + ${halfRam} = ${gx}`);
    const issue = !Number.isInteger(gx) || gx < 0;
    if(issue) lines.push('⚠ result not a non-negative integer — check that Σ(e_p−1) is even and ramification is consistent');
    out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
    out.style.color = issue ? 'var(--pink)' : 'var(--green)';
  }

  go.addEventListener('click', compute);
  [nEl,gyEl,epsEl].forEach(el => el.addEventListener('keydown', e => { if(e.key==='Enter') compute(); }));
  for(const b of exBtns){
    b.addEventListener('click', () => {
      const ex = examples[b.dataset.ex];
      nEl.value = ex.n; gyEl.value = ex.gy; epsEl.value = ex.eps;
      compute();
      out.textContent += `\n\nnote: ${ex.note}`;
    });
  }
  compute();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
One of the key uses of Riemann–Hurwitz: given any compact Riemann surface $X$ of genus $g\ge 1$, a non-constant meromorphic function $f\in K(X)$ realises $X$ as a branched cover of $\mathbb{CP}^1$, and the formula constrains the possible genera and degrees.

Genus as the universal topological invariant

For a compact oriented surface, the genus $g\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$ is literally the number of handles: $g=0$ is the sphere, $g=1$ the torus, $g=2$ the two-holed donut, and so on. It is a homeomorphism invariant, but on a Riemann surface it is also what every complex-analytic number is secretly counting. The standard dictionary:

  • Euler characteristic $\chi(X) = 2 - 2g$.
  • Betti numbers $b_0 = b_2 = 1$, $b_1 = 2g$, so $H_1(X;\mathbb{Z})\cong \mathbb{Z}^{2g}$.
  • Fundamental group $\pi_1(X) = \langle a_1,b_1,\ldots,a_g,b_g\mid \prod[a_i,b_i]=1\rangle$.
  • Holomorphic $1$-forms $\dim_\mathbb{C} H^0(X,\Omega^1) = g$.
  • Canonical divisor degree $\deg K_X = 2g-2$.

All of these are manifestly different-flavoured objects — two pieces of real homology, one complex-analytic dimension, one algebro-geometric degree — and yet all are rigidly determined by the single number $g$. That coincidence is the reason Riemann surfaces occupy a miraculous corner of mathematics: the topology is so restrictive that it pins down the rest.

Riemann–Hurwitz is the first incarnation of this rigidity: once $g_Y$, the degree $n$, and the ramification profile are fixed, $g_X$ is forced. In §6 we will see Riemann–Roch, which is the same rigidity dressed up as an equation for $\ell(D)$ in terms of $g$.

See also
  • Fundamental group

</section>
<!-- end section rh -->

5. Uniformization

Every Riemann surface $X$ has a universal cover $\widetilde X$, and $\widetilde X$ inherits a Riemann-surface structure making $\widetilde X \to X$ holomorphic. The deepest theorem in the subject — the uniformization theorem of Koebe and Poincaré — identifies the list of possible $\widetilde X$ up to biholomorphism.

Every simply connected Riemann surface is biholomorphic to exactly one of $$\mathbb{CP}^1,\quad \mathbb{C},\quad \mathbb{H} = \{\tau\in\mathbb{C}:\mathrm{Im}\,\tau > 0\}.$$ Hence every Riemann surface $X$ is a quotient $\widetilde X / \Gamma$ where $\Gamma = \pi_1(X)$ acts freely and properly discontinuously by biholomorphisms of $\widetilde X$.

The three universal covers correspond to three geometric signatures:

  • $\mathbb{CP}^1$ → spherical: genus $0$, positive curvature.
  • $\mathbb{C}$ → Euclidean: genus $1$ tori $\mathbb{C}/\Lambda$, flat.
  • $\mathbb{H}$ → hyperbolic: genus $\ge 2$ and most non-compact surfaces, negative curvature.

The trichotomy three worlds one list
positive curvature · genus 0

$\mathbb{CP}^1$ only

One point: only $\mathbb{CP}^1$ itself covers $\mathbb{CP}^1$. Automorphism group $\mathrm{PGL}_2(\mathbb{C})$.
flat · genus 1

$\mathbb{C}/\Lambda$

Every torus. Moduli space $\mathbb{H}/\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$.
negative curvature · g ≥ 2

$\mathbb{H}/\Gamma$

The "most surfaces" case. For genus $g\ge 2$, Teichmüller dim $= 6g-6$.

blocks.html:
Some flavour of the hyperbolic case: a surface of genus $g\ge 2$ has universal cover $\mathbb{H}$, and the deck group $\Gamma\subset\mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathbb{R})$ is a Fuchsian group. The surface is tiled by a fundamental polygon (for genus $g$, a regular $4g$-gon with side identifications $a_1 b_1 a_1^{-1} b_1^{-1} \cdots a_g b_g a_g^{-1} b_g^{-1}$); the card above shows the $4\cdot 2 = 8$-gon for genus $2$. This is the hyperbolic-geometry face of the moduli space $\mathcal{M}_g$.

The Gauss–Bonnet check

The trichotomy is enforced by a curvature integral. If $X$ carries a metric of constant curvature $\kappa$ compatible with its complex structure, Gauss–Bonnet says $\int_X \kappa\,dA = 2\pi\chi(X) = 2\pi(2-2g)$. For $g=0$, the integral is positive and $\kappa > 0$; for $g=1$ it vanishes and $\kappa = 0$; for $g\ge 2$ it is negative and $\kappa < 0$. Uniformization is the statement that such a metric always exists and is unique in its conformal class, up to the obvious symmetries.

Used in
Riemann surfaces · Divisors and the Riemann–Roch theorem

6. Divisors and a peek at Riemann–Roch

A divisor on a Riemann surface $X$ is a formal $\mathbb{Z}$-linear sum $D = \sum_{P\in X} n_P \cdot P$ with $n_P = 0$ for all but finitely many $P$. The degree of $D$ is $\deg D = \sum n_P\in\mathbb{Z}$. Divisors form the free abelian group $\mathrm{Div}(X)$; the subgroup of principal divisors $(f) = \sum \mathrm{ord}_P(f)\cdot P$ of meromorphic functions is the kernel of the degree (on a compact $X$) on the level of equivalence classes, giving the divisor class group (Picard group) $\mathrm{Pic}(X) = \mathrm{Div}(X)/\mathrm{princ}$.

Divisors are the algebraic bookkeeping for "orders of zero and pole" and show up everywhere: every line bundle $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ is $\mathcal{O}(D)$ for some divisor $D$, and the degree of the line bundle equals $\deg D$.

Place points, build a divisor left-click adds +1, right-click (or shift-click) adds −1

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = document.getElementById('svg-div');
  const out = document.getElementById('out-div');
  const clearBtn = document.getElementById('div-clear');
  if(!svg) return;

  const cx = 380, cy = 130, R = 80;
  // draw a torus-shape outline: big ellipse with inner hole ellipse (top-down)
  svg.appendChild(SVG('ellipse',{cx,cy,rx:R*2.1,ry:R*0.85,fill:'#101722',stroke:'var(--violet)','stroke-width':1.3}));
  svg.appendChild(SVG('ellipse',{cx,cy,rx:R*0.85,ry:R*0.28,fill:'#0b0f16',stroke:'var(--violet)','stroke-width':1.0,'stroke-dasharray':'3 3'}));
  const title = SVG('text',{x:cx,y:cy-R*0.92-8,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,fill:'var(--mute)'});
  title.textContent = 'a compact Riemann surface (genus 1, top view)';
  svg.appendChild(title);
  // sensitive region is the annulus
  const gPts = SVG('g');
  svg.appendChild(gPts);

  let points = []; // {x,y,n} screen coords and ±1 multiplicity label
  let idCounter = 1;

  function isOnSurface(x,y){
    // inside outer ellipse AND outside inner ellipse
    const u1 = (x-cx)/(R*2.1), v1 = (y-cy)/(R*0.85);
    const u2 = (x-cx)/(R*0.85), v2 = (y-cy)/(R*0.28);
    return (u1*u1+v1*v1 <= 1) && (u2*u2+v2*v2 >= 1);
  }

  function render(){
    while(gPts.firstChild) gPts.removeChild(gPts.firstChild);
    // count degree
    let deg = 0;
    const counts = new Map(); // by id → count? We just show all labels.
    for(const p of points){
      const color = p.n > 0 ? 'var(--green)' : 'var(--pink)';
      gPts.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:p.x,cy:p.y,r:6,fill:color,stroke:'#111','stroke-width':1.3}));
      const label = SVG('text',{x:p.x,y:p.y-10,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':11,fill:color,'font-weight':600});
      label.textContent = (p.n > 0 ? '+' : '') + p.n + '·P' + p.id;
      gPts.appendChild(label);
      deg += p.n;
    }

    // build readout
    if(!points.length){
      out.textContent = 'D = 0   ·   deg D = 0\nclick on the ring to place points';
      out.style.color = 'var(--mute)';
    } else {
      const terms = points.map(p => (p.n>0?'+':'−')+'1·P'+p.id).join(' ');
      // genus 1: Riemann–Roch gives ℓ(D) − ℓ(K − D) = deg D, since g = 1
      // For simple cases: deg D < 0 ⇒ ℓ(D)=0; deg D > 0 ⇒ ℓ(D) = deg D (if K−D has negative degree).
      const g = 1;
      let ell = '';
      if(deg < 0) ell = `ℓ(D) = 0 (degree negative on genus 1)`;
      else if(deg === 0) ell = `ℓ(D) ∈ {0,1} (depends on whether D is principal)`;
      else ell = `ℓ(D) = ${deg} (generic divisor on genus-1 surface, deg K = 0)`;

      out.textContent =
        `D = ${terms}\n` +
        `deg D = ${deg}   (distinct points: ${points.length})\n` +
        `Riemann–Roch (g = ${g}): ℓ(D) − ℓ(K−D) = deg D − g + 1 = ${deg} − 1 + 1 = ${deg}\n` +
        `       ${ell}`;
      out.style.color = 'var(--ink)';
    }
  }

  function addAt(x,y,n){
    if(!isOnSurface(x,y)) return;
    points.push({x,y,n,id:idCounter++});
    render();
  }

  svg.addEventListener('click', e => {
    const r = svg.getBoundingClientRect();
    const sx = (e.clientX - r.left)*(760/r.width);
    const sy = (e.clientY - r.top)*(240/r.height);
    const sign = (e.shiftKey || e.button === 2) ? -1 : 1;
    addAt(sx, sy, sign);
  });
  svg.addEventListener('contextmenu', e => {
    e.preventDefault();
    const r = svg.getBoundingClientRect();
    const sx = (e.clientX - r.left)*(760/r.width);
    const sy = (e.clientY - r.top)*(240/r.height);
    addAt(sx, sy, -1);
  });
  clearBtn.addEventListener('click', () => { points = []; idCounter = 1; render(); });
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
The central result relating divisors to complex analysis is:

Riemann–Roch. Let $X$ be a compact Riemann surface of genus $g$, $K$ a canonical divisor (i.e. the divisor of any meromorphic $1$-form), and $D$ any divisor on $X$. Then $$ \ell(D) - \ell(K - D) \;=\; \deg D - g + 1, $$ where $\ell(D) = \dim_\mathbb{C} H^0(X, \mathcal{O}(D))$ is the dimension of the space of meromorphic functions $f$ with $(f) + D \ge 0$.

Two quick consequences, just to feel it:

  • Genus $0$. On $\mathbb{CP}^1$, $g=0$ and $K = -2\cdot \infty$. For $D = n\cdot \infty$ we get $\ell(D) = n+1$: polynomials of degree $\le n$.
  • Genus $1$. On an elliptic curve, $\deg K = 2g-2 = 0$ and in fact $K \sim 0$. For $\deg D > 0$, $\ell(K-D)=0$ and $\ell(D) = \deg D$: this is where the theory of elliptic functions with prescribed poles lives.

The qualitative content: $\ell(D) \ge \deg D - g + 1$ always, with equality when $\deg D$ is large enough. The correction term $\ell(K-D)$ is what makes the theorem powerful — it encodes exactly how the first-order count fails, and the answer is that it fails by sections of the canonical bundle twisted by $-D$.

A worked example on a genus-$1$ surface

Fix an elliptic curve $E = \mathbb{C}/\Lambda$ with origin $O$ (the identity of the group law). On $E$ we have $g=1$, $K \sim 0$, and Abel's theorem identifies $\mathrm{Pic}^0(E)$ with $E$ itself. Consider divisors of the form $D_n = n\cdot O$ for $n\ge 1$.

$D$$\deg D$$\ell(D)$basis of $L(D)$
$0$$0$$1$$\{1\}$
$O$$1$$1$$\{1\}$ — no nonconstant function with a single simple pole
$2\cdot O$$2$$2$$\{1, \wp\}$ — the Weierstrass $\wp$
$3\cdot O$$3$$3$$\{1, \wp, \wp'\}$
$n\cdot O\;(n\ge 1)$$n$$n$polynomials of degree $\le n$ in $\wp,\wp'$ with poles only at $O$

Row 2 is the tell-tale feature of non-zero genus: even though $\deg D = 1 > 0 = g - 1$, you do not get a section with a simple pole at a single prescribed point (such a function would be a biholomorphism $E\to\mathbb{CP}^1$). The Riemann–Roch formula records this as the case where $\ell(K-D) = \ell(-O)$ jumps — except $\ell(-O) = 0$, and the correction is hidden in the subtler statement that $1\cdot O$ is not principal (that's Abel's theorem again: $1\cdot O - 0$ is zero in $\mathrm{Pic}^0$ iff $O = O$, trivially, but not in the sense of "arising from a function"). For genus $0$ the analogous phenomenon doesn't happen: $1\cdot \infty$ is the divisor of $1/z$ (taking "negative of the zero of $z$" with the right conventions), and $\ell(1\cdot\infty) = 2$.

See also
  • Holomorphic functions
Used in
Moduli spaces · Higher-genus moduli and stable curves

</section>
<!-- end section divisors -->

7. Connections

Riemann surfaces glue together the rest of the mathematics in the notebook.

Differential forms and holomorphic $1$-forms

The sheaf $\Omega^1_X$ of holomorphic $1$-forms on a compact Riemann surface $X$ of genus $g$ has $H^0(X,\Omega^1) = g$-dimensional. This is the Hodge / de Rham content: the canonical line bundle $K_X = \Omega^1_X$ has degree $2g-2$, and its global sections give Abel–Jacobi maps and the period lattice.

Sheaf cohomology and Serre duality

Riemann–Roch is the Euler-characteristic identity $\chi(\mathcal{O}(D)) = \deg D - g + 1$ together with Serre duality $H^1(X,\mathcal{O}(D))^* \cong H^0(X,\Omega^1(-D))$. The whole machinery of sheaf cohomology is forced onto you by trying to make the counting work.

Elliptic curves and modular forms

A compact Riemann surface of genus $1$ with a chosen base point is precisely an elliptic curve. The moduli space $\mathcal{M}_{1,1} = \mathbb{H}/\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ of such objects is the home of modular forms; meromorphic sections of powers of the Hodge bundle over $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ are modular forms.

Algebraic geometry and schemes

Every compact Riemann surface is the complex points of a unique smooth projective algebraic curve over $\mathbb{C}$, and this equivalence is functorial: the category of compact Riemann surfaces with non-constant holomorphic maps is equivalent to the category of smooth projective curves with non-constant morphisms. Push further: arbitrary Riemann surfaces correspond to smooth $1$-dimensional complex-analytic spaces, and the right setting to generalise over arbitrary base is the theory of arithmetic surfaces and the étale site.

Topology

The underlying topology — genus, the fundamental group $\pi_1 = \langle a_1,b_1,\ldots,a_g,b_g \mid \prod [a_i,b_i]\rangle$, the mapping class group — controls everything non-analytic. Teichmüller theory lifts the moduli space $\mathcal{M}_g$ to a contractible space $\mathcal{T}_g\cong\mathbb{R}^{6g-6}$ on which the mapping class group acts.

Where to next

A reasonable next step is to pin down any one of these faces with more care:

  • Analytic: work through Forster's Lectures on Riemann surfaces, or Donaldson's treatment — the sheaf-theoretic proof of Riemann–Roch, Abel–Jacobi, and the Jacobian.
  • Algebraic: Hartshorne IV, or Vakil's chapter on curves — the same theorems in the language of schemes, degenerations, and moduli.
  • Hyperbolic: Farb–Margalit, Primer on mapping class groups, to see how Teichmüller space, Dehn twists, and the curve complex encode the topology of $\mathcal{M}_g$.

None of the three perspectives makes the others obsolete. You need the whole triangle to do real work.

The slogan to take away: a compact Riemann surface is a single object with three faces — a complex manifold, an algebraic curve, and a hyperbolic (or Euclidean, or spherical) geometry — and the subject consists of theorems that let you translate freely between those faces.

Next doors in the notebook: elliptic curves (for the genus-$1$ story), sheaf cohomology (for the machinery behind Riemann–Roch), and algebraic geometry / schemes (for the arithmetic generalisation).
